Description (Layman): All images, icons, and non-text content must have a text alternative.
Instructions for Product Owners: Add alt text to images, icons, and buttons. Use descriptive text for decorative images if they convey meaning.
Simple Example: An image of a cat has alt="Gray tabby cat sleeping on a windowsill."

Description (Layman): Audio-only and video-only content must have a text alternative.
Instructions for Product Owners: Provide a transcript for audio-only, and a description for video-only content.
Simple Example: A podcast episode has a text transcript below the audio player.

Description (Layman): Videos with spoken content must have captions.
Instructions for Product Owners: Add synchronized captions to all videos with audio.
Simple Example: A lecture video has captions at the bottom.

Description (Layman): Videos with important visual information not in the audio must have an audio description or text alternative.
Instructions for Product Owners: Provide an audio description track or a text alternative for visual-only information in videos.
Simple Example: A video of a science experiment includes an audio track describing the steps.

Description (Layman): Live videos with audio must have captions.
Instructions for Product Owners: Provide live captions for live-streamed events.
Simple Example: A live news broadcast has real-time captions.

Description (Layman): Pre-recorded videos with important visual information must have an audio description.
Instructions for Product Owners: Provide an audio description track for videos with visual-only content.
Simple Example: A video tutorial includes an audio description of what’s happening on screen.

Description (Layman): Headings, lists, and form fields must be marked up so their structure is clear to assistive technology.
Instructions for Product Owners: Use proper HTML tags for headings, lists, and form fields.
Simple Example: Use<h1>for main headings and<label>for form fields.

Description (Layman): Content must be presented in a logical order that makes sense when read aloud.
Instructions for Product Owners: Arrange content so it flows logically, even when read by a screen reader.
Simple Example: A news article has the headline first, followed by the body text.

Description (Layman): Instructions must not rely solely on shape, size, or location.
Instructions for Product Owners: Include text descriptions for instructions, not just "click the button on the left."
Simple Example: "Click the 'Submit' button at the bottom of the form."

Description (Layman): Content must work in both portrait and landscape modes unless essential.
Instructions for Product Owners: Avoid locking screen orientation; let users rotate their device.
Simple Example: A form can be filled out in both portrait and landscape on a mobile device.

Description (Layman): Form fields must be identified so assistive technology knows what to expect.
Instructions for Product Owners: Useautocompleteandaria-labelfor form fields.
Simple Example: A "Name" field usesautocomplete="name".

Description (Layman): Color must not be the only way to convey information.
Instructions for Product Owners: Use text or icons in addition to color to indicate status.
Simple Example: "Required fields are marked with an asterisk and the word 'Required.'"

Description (Layman): Users must be able to pause or stop audio that plays automatically for more than 3 seconds.
Instructions for Product Owners: Provide a pause/stop button for any auto-playing audio.
Simple Example: A background music track has a visible stop button.

Description (Layman): Text must have sufficient contrast with its background.
Instructions for Product Owners: Use a contrast ratio of at least 4.5:1 for normal text.
Simple Example: Black text on white background.

Description (Layman): Users must be able to resize text up to 200% without loss of content or function.
Instructions for Product Owners: Test your site with text zoomed to 200%.
Simple Example: All content remains readable and usable when zoomed in.

Description (Layman): Avoid using images of text unless necessary.
Instructions for Product Owners: Use real text instead of images of text whenever possible.
Simple Example: Use a heading tag instead of an image with text inside.

Description (Layman): Content must reflow and not require horizontal scrolling when zoomed.
Instructions for Product Owners: Ensure content adapts to screen size and zoom.
Simple Example: A webpage fits on a mobile screen without horizontal scrolling.

Description (Layman): UI components and graphics must have sufficient contrast.
Instructions for Product Owners: Use a contrast ratio of at least 3:1 for icons and controls.
Simple Example: Navigation buttons have a dark border on a light background.

Description (Layman): Users must be able to adjust text spacing for readability.
Instructions for Product Owners: Allow users to override text spacing in CSS.
Simple Example: User styles can increase line height and letter spacing.

Description (Layman): Additional content on hover/focus must be dismissible, hoverable, and persistent.
Instructions for Product Owners: Make sure pop-ups can be dismissed without moving the pointer.
Simple Example: A tooltip appears on hover and stays until dismissed.

Description (Layman): All functionality must be available via keyboard.
Instructions for Product Owners: Test all features with keyboard only (Tab, Enter, Arrow keys).
Simple Example: Users can navigate a form and submit it with just the keyboard.

Description (Layman): Users must not get stuck in any part of the site using keyboard navigation.
Instructions for Product Owners: Ensure Tab and Shift+Tab always move focus forward and backward.
Simple Example: After opening a modal, users can close it with the keyboard.

Description (Layman): If single-key shortcuts are used, users must be able to turn them off or change them.
Instructions for Product Owners: Allow users to disable or remap single-key shortcuts.
Simple Example: A user can disable the "S" key shortcut for search.

Description (Layman): Users must be able to extend or turn off time limits.
Instructions for Product Owners: Provide options to adjust or disable time limits.
Simple Example: A quiz allows extra time or can be paused.

Description (Layman): Users must be able to pause, stop, or hide moving content.
Instructions for Product Owners: Provide controls for moving, blinking, or scrolling content.
Simple Example: A carousel has pause and next/previous buttons.

Description (Layman): Content must not flash more than three times per second.
Instructions for Product Owners: Avoid rapid flashing animations or content.
Simple Example: A banner ad does not flash rapidly.

Description (Layman): Users must be able to skip repeated content.
Instructions for Product Owners: Add a "Skip to Content" link at the top of each page.
Simple Example: A "Skip to main content" link is the first focusable element.

Description (Layman): Pages must have descriptive titles.
Instructions for Product Owners: Give each page a unique, descriptive title.
Simple Example: "Contact Us – My Company" as the page title.

Description (Layman): The tab order must be logical and meaningful.
Instructions for Product Owners: Arrange interactive elements in a logical order.
Simple Example: Tab moves from top to bottom, left to right.

Description (Layman): The purpose of each link must be clear from its text or context.
Instructions for Product Owners: Use descriptive link text or provide context.
Simple Example: "Read our privacy policy" instead of "Click here."

Description (Layman): Users must have multiple ways to find pages.
Instructions for Product Owners: Provide a search, sitemap, or navigation menu.
Simple Example: A site has both a menu and a search bar.

Description (Layman): Headings and labels must be clear and descriptive.
Instructions for Product Owners: Use meaningful headings and labels for all content.
Simple Example: "Product Features" as a heading above a feature list.

Description (Layman): Keyboard focus must be clearly visible.
Instructions for Product Owners: Highlight focused elements with a visible border or background.
Simple Example: A blue outline appears around a focused button.

Description (Layman): Focused elements must not be hidden by other content.
Instructions for Product Owners: Ensure focused items are visible, even with sticky headers.
Simple Example: Tabbing to a link does not hide it behind a fixed menu.

Description (Layman): Complex gestures must have a single-pointer alternative.
Instructions for Product Owners: Provide single-click or tap alternatives for gestures.
Simple Example: A map can be zoomed with a button, not just a pinch gesture.

Description (Layman): Users must be able to cancel pointer actions.
Instructions for Product Owners: Allow users to undo or cancel actions triggered by a click or tap.
Simple Example: A dragged item can be canceled before dropping.

Description (Layman): The accessible name must match the visible label.
Instructions for Product Owners: Ensure screen readers read the same text as the visible label.
Simple Example: A button labeled "Submit" has an accessible name of "Submit."

Description (Layman): Functionality must not rely on device motion.
Instructions for Product Owners: Provide alternative controls for motion-based features.
Simple Example: A game can be controlled with buttons, not just by tilting the device.

Description (Layman): Dragging actions must have a simple alternative.
Instructions for Product Owners: Offer buttons or keyboard controls for drag-and-drop.
Simple Example: A list can be reordered with up/down arrows, not just by dragging.

Description (Layman): Clickable targets must be at least 24x24 pixels.
Instructions for Product Owners: Increase button and link size; add padding if needed.
Simple Example: Small icons are enlarged to at least 24x24 pixels.

Description (Layman): The page language must be identified.
Instructions for Product Owners: Set thelangattribute on the HTML element.
Simple Example: <html lang="en">for an English page.

Description (Layman): Changes in language must be marked for assistive technology.
Instructions for Product Owners: Use thelangattribute for content in a different language.
Simple Example: A French quote on an English page:Bonjour.

Description (Layman): Changing focus must not trigger unexpected actions.
Instructions for Product Owners: Only trigger actions when users intentionally interact with controls.
Simple Example: Pressing Tab does not submit a form.

Description (Layman): Changing settings must not cause unexpected changes.
Instructions for Product Owners: Only trigger changes when users explicitly submit or confirm.
Simple Example: Selecting a checkbox does not submit a form automatically.

Description (Layman): Navigation must be consistent across pages.
Instructions for Product Owners: Keep menus and navigation in the same order and location.
Simple Example: The main menu is always at the top of every page.

Description (Layman): Components with the same function must be identified consistently.
Instructions for Product Owners: Use the same icons, labels, and styling for the same actions.
Simple Example: All "Download" buttons look and work the same.

Description (Layman): Help options must be in a consistent location.
Instructions for Product Owners: Place help links or buttons in the same spot on every page.
Simple Example: A "Help" link is always in the top-right corner.

Description (Layman): Errors must be clearly identified.
Instructions for Product Owners: Show clear error messages near the problematic field.
Simple Example: "Please enter a valid email address" appears below the email field.

Description (Layman): Form fields must have clear labels or instructions.
Instructions for Product Owners: Add visible labels for every input; don’t rely on placeholder text.
Simple Example: "First Name" label above the input box.

Description (Layman): Users must receive suggestions for correcting errors.
Instructions for Product Owners: Provide helpful suggestions in error messages.
Simple Example: "Password must be at least 8 characters" if a short password is entered.

Description (Layman): Users must be able to review and confirm important actions.
Instructions for Product Owners: Add a review step or confirmation prompt for critical actions.
Simple Example: Before submitting a payment, show a summary and ask for confirmation.

Description (Layman): Context-sensitive help must be available for complex forms.
Instructions for Product Owners: Provide help text or links for complex inputs.
Simple Example: A question mark icon next to a field explains what to enter.

Description (Layman): For all forms, users must be able to review, correct, and confirm before submission.
Instructions for Product Owners: Allow users to review and edit before final submission.
Simple Example: A form shows a summary before the final submit button.

Description (Layman): Users must not be asked to re-enter information unless necessary.
Instructions for Product Owners: Autofill repeated fields or remember previous entries.
Simple Example: Shipping address auto-fills if already provided as billing address.

Description (Layman): Authentication must not require cognitive function tests unless an alternative is provided.
Instructions for Product Owners: Allow password managers, copy-paste, or alternative authentication.
Simple Example: Users can paste passwords or use a password manager.

Description (Layman): Markup must be well-formed and valid.
Instructions for Product Owners: Use valid HTML and avoid duplicate IDs or malformed tags.
Simple Example: No duplicate IDs in the code.

Description (Layman): Custom controls must have proper names, roles, and values.
Instructions for Product Owners: Use ARIA labels and roles for custom controls.
Simple Example: A custom button usesrole="button"andaria-label="Submit".

Description (Layman): Status messages must be announced to assistive technology.
Instructions for Product Owners: Use ARIA live regions for dynamic updates.
Simple Example: "Your form was submitted successfully" is announced by screen readers.
